title: Understand associative property of multiplication
description: Practice changing the grouping of factors in multiplication problems to find equivalent expressions.
topic_path: Get ready for equations, expressions, and inequalities

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which two expressions are equivalent to ${(9 \times 2) \times 5}$ ?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Does $\blueD{(9 \times 2) \times 5}=\greenD{9 \times (2 \times 5)}?$

Yes! When multiplying, we can group numbers differently by moving the parentheses and still arrive at the same answer. [[☃ explanation 1]]

This is called the **associative property of multiplication**.

Does $\blueD{(9 \times 2) \times 5}=\maroonD{11 \times 5}?$

$
\begin{align}
\blueD{(9 \times 2) \times 5} &\stackrel{?}{=}  \maroonD{11 \times 5} \\\\
\blueD{18 \times 5} &\neq  \maroonD{11 \times 5}
 \end{align}$

No, $\blueD{(9 \times 2) \times 5}$ is not equivalent to $\maroonD{11 \times 5}$.

The following expressions are equivalent to $\blueD{(9 \times 2) \times 5}$:

* $\greenD{9 \times (2 \times 5)}$

* $\purpleC{9 \times 10}$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which two expressions are equivalent to ${(9 \times 3) \times 3}$ ?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Does $\blueD{(9 \times 3) \times 3}=\greenD{9 \times (3 \times 3)}?$

Yes! When multiplying, we can group numbers differently by moving the parentheses and still arrive at the same answer. [[☃ explanation 1]]

This is called the **associative property of multiplication**.

Does $\blueD{(9 \times 3) \times 3}=\maroonD{27 + 3}?$

$
\begin{align}
\blueD{(9 \times 3) \times 3} &\stackrel{?}{=}  \maroonD{27 +  3} \\\\
\blueD{27 \times 3} &\neq  \maroonD{27 +3}
 \end{align}$

No, $\blueD{(9 \times 3) \times 3}$ is not equivalent to $\maroonD{27 +3}$.

The following expressions are equivalent to $\blueD{(9 \times 3) \times 3}$:

* $\greenD{9 \times (3 \times 3)}$

* $\purpleC{9 \times 9}$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which two expressions are equivalent to ${(6 \times 2) \times 5}$ ?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Does $\blueD{(6 \times 2) \times 5}=\purpleC{12 \times 5}?$

$
\begin{align}
\blueD{(6 \times 2) \times 5} &\stackrel{?}{=}  \purpleC{12 \times 5} \\\\
\blueD{12 \times 5} &=  \purpleC{12 \times 5}
 \end{align}$

Yes, $\blueD{(6 \times 2) \times 5}$ is equivalent to $\purpleC{12 \times 5}$.

Does $\blueD{(6 \times 2) \times 5}=\maroonD{12 \times 10}?$

$
\begin{align}
\blueD{(6 \times 2) \times 5} &\stackrel{?}{=}  \maroonD{12 \times  10} \\\\
\blueD{12 \times 5} &\neq  \maroonD{12 \times 10}
 \end{align}$

No, $\blueD{(6 \times 2) \times 5}$ is not equivalent to $\maroonD{12 \times 10}$.

Does $\blueD{(6 \times 2) \times 5}=\greenD{6 \times (2 \times 5)}?$

Yes! When multiplying, we can group numbers differently by moving the parentheses and still arrive at the same answer.  [[☃ explanation 1]]

This is called the **associative property of multiplication**.

The following expressions are equivalent to $\blueD{(6 \times 2) \times 5}$:

* $\greenD{6 \times (2 \times 5)}$

* $\purpleC{12 \times 5}$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which two expressions are equivalent to ${5 \times (5 \times 2)}$ ?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Does $\blueD{5 \times (5 \times 2)}=\maroonD{(5 \times 5) \times (5 \times 2)} ?$

$
\begin{align}
\blueD{5 \times (5 \times 2)} &\stackrel{?}{=}  \maroonD{(5 \times 5) \times (5 \times 2)} \\\\
\blueD{5 \times 10} &\neq  \maroonD{25 \times 10}
 \end{align}$

No, $\blueD{5 \times (5 \times 2)}$ is not equivalent to $\maroonD{(5 \times 5) \times (5 \times 2)} $.

Does $\blueD{5 \times (5 \times 2)}=\greenD{(5 \times 5) \times 2}?$

Yes! When multiplying, we can group numbers differently by moving the parentheses and still arrive at the same answer. [[☃ explanation 1]]

This is called the **associative property of multiplication**.

Does $\blueD{5 \times (5 \times 2)}=\purpleC{25 \times 2}?$

$
\begin{align}
\blueD{5 \times (5 \times 2)} &\stackrel{?}{=}  \purpleC{25 \times 2}\\\\
\blueD{(5 \times 5) \times 2} &\stackrel{?}{=}  \purpleC{25 \times 2}&&\text{Associative property of multiplication}\\\\
\blueD{25 \times 2} &=  \purpleC{25 \times 2}
 \end{align}$

Yes, $\blueD{5 \times (5 \times 2)}$ is equivalent to $\purpleC{25 \times 2}$.

The following expressions are equivalent to $\blueD{5 \times (5 \times 2)}$:

* $\greenD{(5 \times 5) \times 2}$

* $\purpleC{25 \times 2}$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which two expressions are equivalent to ${7 \times 3 \times 3}$ ?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Does $\blueD{7 \times 3\times 3}=\maroonD{(7 \times 3) \times 3} ?$

Yes! The parentheses in the second part of the equation just tell us which numbers to multiply first.

The following expressions are equivalent to $\blueD{7 \times 3 \times3}$:

* $\maroonD{(7 \times 3) \times 3}$

* $\purpleC{7 \times 9}$
To find the product of $\greenD{12}\times\blueD{4}$, we can break $\greenD{12}$ into smaller factors.

We want to break $\greenD{12} $ into groups of $\greenD{2}$.

$\greenD{12} \times \blueD{4} ={} ?\times \greenD{2} \times \blueD{4}$

$\greenD{12}$ is the same as $\greenD6$ groups of $\greenD2$.

$\greenD{12} \times \blueD{4} ={} \fbox{\greenD6}\times \greenD{2} \times \blueD{4}$

$\greenD{12}$ groups of $\blueD{4}$ 

![12 groups of 4](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/d651cc146b29a971393064c5723467e484051d65)

and $\greenD6$ groups of $\greenD2$ groups of $\blueD{4}$ 

![The same dots in 6 groups of 2 groups of 4](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/d52acaabba969e833974d95f646e1cef2da194c4)

have the same total.
$12 \times 4 = 6 \times 2 \times 4$
To find the product of $\greenD{20} \times \blueD{2}$, we can break $\greenD{20}$ into smaller factors.

We want to break $\greenD{20} $ into $\greenD{5}$ groups.

$\greenD{20} \times \blueD{2} =\greenD{5} \times {?} \times \blueD{2}$

$\greenD{20}$ is the same as $\greenD{5}$ groups of $\greenD{4}$.

$\greenD{20} \times \blueD{2} =\greenD{5} \times \fbox{\greenD{4}} \times \blueD{2}$

$\greenD{20}$ groups of $\blueD{2}$ 

![20 groups of 2](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/8c8093f315364cc8a62e8160c5c4d6947fd66870)

and $\greenD{5}$ groups of $\greenD{4}$ groups of $\blueD{2}$ 

![The same dots in 5 groups of 4 groups of 2](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/97b3274d52d272a5ad0082bb198231c8cf39216b)

have the same total.
$20 \times 2 = 5 \times 4 \times 2$
To find the product of $\blueD{3} \times \greenD{15}$, we can break $\greenD{15}$ into smaller factors.

We want to break $\greenD{15} $ into $\greenD{3}$ groups.

$\blueD{3} \times \greenD{15} =\blueD{3} \times \greenD{3} \times {?}$

$\greenD{15}$ is the same as $\greenD{3}$ groups of $\greenD{5}$.

$\blueD{3} \times \greenD{15} =\blueD{3} \times \greenD{3} \times \fbox{\greenD{5}}$

$\blueD{3}$ groups of $\greenD{15}$ 

![3 groups of 15](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/44e3a3fd25a3ca53227afec9a6c87b1bc685457a)

and $\blueD{3}$ groups of $\greenD{3}$ groups of $\greenD{5}$ 

![The same dots in 3 groups of 3 groups of 5](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/fa2c372d381e54ce9d60a6f8b364ae8cf963a68b)

have the same total.
$3 \times 15 = 3 \times 3 \times 5$
To find the product of $\blueD{3} \times \greenD{18}$, we can break $\greenD{18}$ into smaller factors.

We want to break $\greenD{18} $ into groups of $\greenD{9}$.

$\blueD{3} \times \greenD{18} =\blueD{3} \times {?} \times \greenD{9}$

$\greenD{18}$ is the same as $\greenD{2}$ groups of $\greenD{9}$.

$\blueD{3} \times \greenD{18} =\blueD{3} \times \fbox{\greenD{2}} \times \greenD{9}$

$\blueD{3}$ groups of $\greenD{18}$ 

![3 groups of 18](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/de87cdb926ff8eb52f3a61172b1400bd13d25dc3)

and $\blueD{3}$ groups of $\greenD{2}$ groups of $\greenD{9}$ 

![The same dots in 3 groups of 2 groups of 9](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/4ed5907cb47616f3b938f5f04a8d1b77addc3021)

have the same total.
$3 \times 18 = 3 \times 2 \times 9$

$\blueD{4} \times (\maroonD{3} \times \greenD{6})$ can represent $\blueD{4}$ groups of $\maroonD{3}$ groups of $\greenD{6}$.

![4 groups of 3 groups of 6 dots](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/980e56677e3bb3b544c3d424454fdc7648c9e27c)

How can we complete $(\blueD{4} \times{\maroonD{?}})\times \greenD{6}$ to get the same total?

We can think of $(\blueD{4} \times{\maroonD{?}})\times \greenD{6}$ as $(\blueD{4} \times {\maroonD{?}})$ groups of $\greenD{6}$.   

![The same dots in 12 groups of 6. The groups are in 4 columns with 3 groups per column](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/39422e954d9577e9dcfceb7564ffa0e45c7898eb)

There are $\purpleC{12}$ groups of $\greenD{6}$, which equals $(\blueD{4} \times {\maroonD{3}})$ groups of $\greenD{6}$.

We can change the grouping of the factors without changing the total.  

[[☃ explanation 1]]
$\blueD{4} \times (\maroonD{3} \times \greenD{6})= (\blueD{4} \times \maroonD{3})\times \greenD{6}$

$\blueD{4} \times (\maroonD{2} \times \greenD{7})$ can represent $\blueD{4}$ groups of $\maroonD{2}$ groups of $\greenD{7}$.

![4 groups of 2 groups of 7 dots](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/92616521209821823ead880db20021ac9d2fda2b)

How can we complete $(\blueD{4} \times{\maroonD{2}})\times {\greenD{?}}$ to get the same total?

We can think of $(\blueD{4} \times{\maroonD{2}})\times \greenD{{?}}$ as $(\blueD{4} \times {\maroonD{2}})$ groups of $\greenD{{?}}$.   

![The same dots in 8 groups of 7. The groups are in 4 columns with 2 groups per column](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/dfdbc48d9e1c8d2762575e718e6191b5d6ed339a)

There are $\purpleC{8}$ groups of $\greenD{7}$, which equals $(\blueD{4} \times {\maroonD{2}})$ groups of $\greenD{7}$.

We can change the grouping of the factors without changing the total.  

[[☃ explanation 1]]
$\blueD{4} \times (\maroonD{2} \times \greenD{7})= (\blueD{4} \times \maroonD{2})\times \greenD{7}$

We can think of $(\blueD{8} \times{\maroonD{3}})\times \greenD{{3}}$ as $(\blueD{8} \times {\maroonD{3}})$ groups of $\greenD{{3}}$. That equals $\purpleC{24}$ groups of $\greenD{3}$.   

![24 groups of 3 dots. The groups are in 8 columns with 3 groups per column](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/81af6ef2bc90530c7e6a35c69fa8530b249741f4)

How can we complete $\blueD{?} \times ({\maroonD{3}}\times {\greenD{3}})$ to get the same total?

$\blueD{?} \times ({\maroonD{3}}\times {\greenD{3}})$ can represent $\blueD{?}$ groups of $\maroonD{3}$ groups of $\greenD{3}$.

![The same dots in 8 groups of 3 groups of 3. ](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/b49567bbaac3be5f0c13ad6e208ff7c7c89552ba)

There are $\blueD{8}$ groups of $\maroonD{3}$ groups of $\greenD{3}$.

We can change the grouping of the factors without changing the total.  

[[☃ explanation 1]]
$(\blueD{8} \times \maroonD{3})\times \greenD{3}= \blueD{8} \times (\maroonD{3} \times \greenD{3})$

We can think of $(\blueD{3} \times{\maroonD{5}})\times \greenD{{2}}$ as $(\blueD{3} \times {\maroonD{5}})$ groups of $\greenD{{2}}$. That equals $\purpleC{15}$ groups of $\greenD{2}$.   

![15 groups of 2 dots. The groups are in 3 columns with 5 groups per column](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/d7d90425a1de6d9abb66baafa1e358eca49d2bd4)

How can we complete $\blueD{3} \times ({\maroonD{?}}\times {\greenD{2}})$ to get the same total?

$\blueD{3} \times ({\maroonD{?}}\times {\greenD{2}})$ can represent $\blueD{3}$ groups of $\maroonD{?}$ groups of $\greenD{2}$.

![The same dots in 3 groups of 5 groups of 2. ](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/713dc4059c8c803c8fd2088c10a3778912eb8f74)

There are $\blueD{3}$ groups of $\maroonD{5}$ groups of $\greenD{2}$.

We can change the grouping of the factors without changing the total.  

[[☃ explanation 1]]
$(\blueD{3} \times \maroonD{5})\times \greenD{2}= \blueD{3} \times (\maroonD{5} \times \greenD{2})$
